topic-map.title: Example: Create a Kubernetes Cluster and Install JCNR
topic-map.shortdesc: This is an example on how to create a Kubernetes cluster and install JCNR.

note.p: To reviewers: Feel free to suggest additions to make this example more interesting and meaningful.

tmbody.p: We present an example on how to create a Kubernetes cluster and install Cloud-Native Router on it. The cluster consists of a single control plane node and a single worker node. We'll install Cloud-Native Router on the single worker node.
We've tested this on VMs running on both a KVM host and a VMWare ESXi host, but the steps can generally apply to bare metal servers and systems with other hypervisors.

In preparation for this example, we've set up 3 VMs, each running a freshly-installed Rocky Linux 8.9 OS (minimal ISO) with ssh root access and the same root password. All VMs run on the same KVM/ESXi host.

entry.p: This is the ansible host where we run kubespray to set up the cluster.
This is the Kubernetes control plane node.
This is the Kubernetes worker node where we'll install JCNR.
unassigned - to be used as a Cloud-Native Router fabric interface
unassigned - to be used as a Cloud-Native Router fabric interface
1This is the tested configuration and not the minimal configuration.
2Interface names may be different on your setup.
3On KVM, this is the device model. On ESXi, this is the adapter type.

4Network settings may be different on your setup. The 10.16.0.254 gateway is the gateway that we set up on the KVM/ESXi host. The 66.129.233.81 DNS server is one of the DNS servers that we use in our lab.

tmbody.p: Furthermore, we've set up the following infrastructure on the KVM/ESXi host:

li.p: A 10.16.0.0/24 network that connects all 3 VMs together on their respective enp1s0 interfaces.

A gateway on the 10.16.0.0/24 network that allows all 3 VMs to reach the Internet using NAT. We don't cover setting up the gateway in this example because this depends largely on the infrastructure you're using.
A second network that the worker node (w1) connects to on the enp2s0 interface.

Default huge page size of 1 GB on the KVM host, with a sufficient number of hugepages that can be allocated to the VMs. In our example, we'll be setting up eight 1-GB hugepages on the worker (w1), so you must configure the KVM host to at least accommodate that.

tmbody.p: shows the setup used in this example. The entire setup is running on a single KVM/ESXi host.

note.p: The rest of this example refers to these VMs as nodes.

taskbody.context:
This procedure performs configuration common to all nodes.
Log in as root to each node and perform the following:

step.cmd: Configure NTP (chrony) to use the appropriate NTP server. In this example, we're using the anyntp.juniper.net server, but you can use any other NTP server recommended for your lab.
step.info: sed -i 's/^pool 2.rocky.pool.ntp.org/server anyntp.juniper.net/' /etc/chrony.confsystemctl restart chronydCheck that the correct NTP server is used.chronyc sources

step.cmd: Stop and disable firewalld.

context.p: Prepare the worker node (w1) for JCNR. The steps in this procedure generally follow what is described in .

In our example, we have a cluster with a single control plane node and a single worker node. We plan to install Cloud-Native Router only on the worker node. That's why we're running this procedure on the worker node (w1) only.

If you have multiple worker nodes in your setup, then perform this procedure on all your worker nodes. If you're running Cloud-Native Router on your control plane node(s), then perform this procedure on your control plane node(s) as well.
Log in as root on worker node (w1) and perform the following:

step.cmd: Set up huge pages.

substep.cmd: Configure grub to allocate eight 1-GB huge pages:
substep.info: echo GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=\"default_hugepagesz=1G hugepagesz=1G hugepages=8 intel_iommu=on iommu=pt\" >> /etc/default/grubEnsure your KVM host has sufficient hugepages to back this request.

substep.cmd: Rocky Linux 8.9 does not come with an update-grub utility, so we'll create one.
substep.info: tee -a /usr/sbin/update-grub << EOF #!/bin/sh set -e exec grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg "$@" EOF chmod +x /usr/sbin/update-grubThis utility simply calls grub2-mkconfig.

substep.cmd: Update grub.

step.cmd: Install iproute.
Configure the OS to load VLAN kernel modules.
Configure the OS to load VFIO kernel modules.
Configure the OS to load the kernel modules required by CRPD.

info.codeblock: tee -a /etc/modules-load.d/crpd.conf << EOF tun fou fou6 ipip ip_tunnel ip6_tunnel mpls_gso mpls_router mpls_iptunnel vrf vxlan EOF

step.cmd: Exclude Cloud-Native Router interfaces from NetworkManager control. In our example, this is the enp2s0 interface. If you have multiple interfaces to exclude, simply add them to the same line and separate each by a space.
step.info: tee -a /etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/crpd.conf << EOF [keyfile] unmanaged-devices+=interface-name:enp2s0 EOFIn our example, the enp1s0 interface attaches to the 10.16.0.0/24 network and the enp2s0 is the Cloud-Native Router fabric interface. We want NetworkManager to control enp1s0 but not enp2s0.

step.cmd: Configure sysctl.conf.

info.codeblock: tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf << EOF kernel.core_pattern=/var/crash/core_%e_%p_%i_%s_%h_%t.gz net.ipv6.conf.default.addr_gen_mode=0 net.ipv6.conf.all.addr_gen_mode=0 net.ipv6.conf.default.autoconf=0 net.ipv6.conf.all.autoconf=0 EOF

step.cmd: Reboot.

task.title: Install Kubespray and Create the Cluster (h1)

taskbody.context: There are different ways to create a Kubernetes cluster. In this example, we'll use kubespray.

step.cmd: Generate an SSH key to use to log in to the cluster nodes.

substep.cmd: Generate an SSH key.
Copy the key to the cluster nodes.

step.cmd: Install git.
Clone the kubespray repository. In this example, we're using kubespray version 2.22.
Install python.
Install the packages required by this version of kubespray. We'll perform this step in a Python virtual environment.

substep.cmd: Set up the virtual environment.
substep.info: cd kubespraypython3 -m venv envsource env/bin/activateThe (env) prepended to your usual Linux command prompt indicates that you're in a virtual environment.

substep.cmd: Install the required packages.

step.info:
Continue in this Python virtual environment for the rest of this procedure.
The remainder of this procedure assumes you're in the kubespray directory.

step.cmd: Use the inventory builder to create the inventory file that describes the cluster. Make sure you specify the correct IP addresses for your setup.
step.info: cp -r inventory/sample inventory/myclusterdeclare -a IPS=(10.16.0.11 10.16.0.14)CONFIG_FILE=inventory/mycluster/hosts.yml python3 contrib/inventory_builder/inventory.py ${IPS[@]}This creates an inventory file in inventory/mycluster called hosts.yml. This is a working inventory file that you can customize to your needs.

step.cmd: Customize the inventory file that you just created.
step.info:
vi inventory/mycluster/hosts.ymlHere's an example of the inventory file we're using:all: hosts: node1: ansible_host: 10.16.0.11 ip: 10.16.0.11 access_ip: 10.16.0.11 node2: ansible_host: 10.16.0.14 ip: 10.16.0.14 access_ip: 10.16.0.14 vars: ansible_host: root host_key_checking: false children: kube_control_plane: hosts: node1: kube_node: hosts: node1: node2: etcd: hosts: node1: k8s_cluster: children: kube_control_plane: kube_node: calico_rr: hosts: {} node1 is the cp1 node and node2 is the w1 node.
We modified the generated inventory file by adding a couple of ansible vars and removing node2 from the kube_control_plane.

step.cmd: Check that ansible can reach and log in to the nodes specified in the inventory file.
Configure the cluster.
step.info: Set the following in inventory/mycluster/group_vars/k8s_cluster/k8s-cluster.yml. The following lines are not contiguous in the file. Look for and modify them in place.kube_network_plugin_multus: true cluster_name: mycluster.local enable_nodelocaldns: false kubeconfig_localhost: true kubectl_localhost: true Keep all other parameters at their defaults.

step.cmd: Create the cluster.
After the cluster comes up (30 mins or more), copy the resulting artifacts to the appropriate directories.

substep.cmd: Copy the kubeconfig to the default kubeconfig directory.
Copy the kubectl binary.

step.cmd: Check that you've copied the artifacts correctly and that the cluster is up.
If everything is up and running, deactivate the Python virtual environment.
step.info: deactivateThe (env) should no longer appear in the Linux command prompt.

taskbody.context:
Prior to installing JCNR, we recommend that you take snapshots of the cluster node VMs (cp1, w1) so you can easily roll back to a clean Kubernetes cluster if the Cloud-Native Router installation goes awry.
The Juniper Networks enterprise-hub repository is enterprise-hub.juniper.net when accessed from outside the Juniper Networks intranet. When accessed from inside Juniper Networks (for example, from our labs), use enterprise-hub.jnpr.net. Make sure to update the enterprise-hub reference in the installation Helm chart values.yaml accordingly (step ).

step.cmd: Download the JCNR Tarball with helm charts from the Juniper Networks Software Download site.

substep.cmd: Install wget.
Follow the instructions on the download site. Ensure you enclose the download URL in double quotes:

step.cmd: Gunzip the downloaded software package.
step.info:
tar -xzvf Juniper_Cloud_Native_Router_24.1-108.tar.gz
Cloud-Native Router release 24.1 is not generally available at the time of the writing of this procedure. Use release 23.4 if release 24.1 is not available. Change into the Juniper_Cloud_Native_Router_24.1-108 directory.cd Juniper_Cloud_Native_Router_24.1-108The rest of this procedure assumes you're in this directory.

step.cmd: Populate secrets/jcnr-secrets.yaml with your base64-encoded root password.

substep.cmd: Encode your root password in base64.
substep.info:
echo <root_password> | base64 -w0where <root_password> is the root password of the nodes (cp1, w1) in your cluster. The output of this command is your base64-encoded root password.
We don't recommend using this method in a real system because the root password will be visible in the Linux command history.

substep.cmd: Copy and paste the output into secrets/jcnr-secrets.yaml.

info.codeblock: root-password: <add your password in base64 format>

step.cmd: Populate secrets/jcnr-secrets.yaml with your base64-encoded CRPD license.
step.info:
Your license might look something like this: Issue Date : yyyy Expiry Date : yyyy HW Serial Number : N/A SSRN : yyyy Feature : yyyy Quantity : 1 License Type : yyyy License Key :
xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx x Encode and copy it into secrets/jcnr-secrets.yaml as follows:

substep.cmd: Create a file that contains the license key on a single line. In this example, we'll name the file license-key-single-line.txt.

info.codeblock: xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx ...

substep.cmd: Encode your single-line license in base64:
substep.info: base64 -w0 license-key-single-line.txtThe output of this command is your base64-encoded license key.

substep.cmd: Copy and paste the output into secrets/jcnr-secrets.yaml.

info.codeblock: crpd-license: | <add your license in base64 format>

step.cmd: Apply the Cloud-Native Router secrets to your cluster.
Encode your enterprise-hub.jnpr.net credentials in base64.

note.p: Follow this step only if you want your cluster to pull Cloud-Native Router images from the enterprise-hub.jnpr.net repository. If you want your cluster to use the images included in the downloaded Cloud-Native Router software package instead, then skip this step and follow the instuctions in .

info.p: The Cloud-Native Router Helm chart uses your enterprise-hub.jnpr.net credentials to pull images from the enterprise-hub.jnpr.net repository.
The Cloud-Native Router Helm chart expects your credentials to be in a specific format. One way of ensuring your credentials are in the proper format is to use docker (podman).

substep.cmd: Install docker (podman).
Create a .docker directory. This is where we'll store our credentials.
Log in to enterprise-hub.jnpr.net.
substep.info: docker login enterprise-hub.jnpr.net --authfile=/root/.docker/config.jsonEnter your enterprise-hub.jnpr.net username and password when prompted. Your credentials are now stored in ~/.docker/config.json.

substep.cmd: Encode your credentials in base64.
substep.info: base64 -w0 ~/.docker/config.jsonThe output of this command is your base64-encoded credentials. You'll use this in the next step.

step.cmd: Configure the main helm chart.
step.info:
In helmchart/values.yaml, look for the following lines and configure as follows:
We're only showing relevant excerpts from helmchart/values.yaml. The lines below are not necessarily contiguous in the file nor should you make them contiguous. Just search for the indicated parameters and modify them in place within the file. Leave other parameters at their default values. registry: enterprise-hub.jnpr.net/ imagePullSecret: registryCredentials: <base64-encoded credentials for enterprise-hub.jnpr.net> secretName: regcred replicas: "1" fabricInterface: - enp2s0: ddp: "off" nodeAffinity: - key: kubernetes.io/hostname operator: In values: - node2 vRouterDeployerPort: 8082 telemetryExporter: metricsPort: 8070 logLevel: info
If you want your cluster to pull images from enterprise-hub.jnpr.net, then replace <base64-encoded credentials for enterprise-hub.jnpr.net> with your base64-encoded enterprise-jnpr.net credentials from the previous step.
If you want your cluster to use the images included in the downloaded Cloud-Native Router software package, comment out imagePullSecret, registryCredentials, and secretName as below (but continue to leave registry: enterprise-hub.jnpr.net/ uncommented): registry: enterprise-hub.jnpr.net/ #imagePullSecret: #registryCredentials: <base64-encoded credentials for enterprise-hub.jnpr.net> #secretName: regcred
The telemetryExporter section is supported starting in release 24.1. Don't add it if you're running release 23.4.

step.cmd: Install the Cloud-Native Router helm chart.

substep.cmd: First, install Helm by downloading the get_helm.sh script to a temporary directory of your choosing.
substep.info: curl -fsSL -o get_helm.sh https://raw.githubusercontent.com/helm/helm/main/scripts/get-helm-3chmod +x get_helm.shRun this script to install Helm../get_helm.sh

substep.cmd: Install JCNR.

step.cmd: If the vrouter doesn't come up after a few minutes, then this may indicate that no iommu is available in your system. Log in to the worker node (w1) and enable noiommu:

substep.cmd: Log in to the worker node (w1) and enable noiommu:
Back on h1, delete the vrouter pod, so that the cluster can create a new one.

step.cmd: Check your cluster again.

info.codeblock: NAMESPACE NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE contrail-deploy contrail-k8s-deployer-59df6699c4-tgpzk 1/1 Running 0 5d contrail contrail-vrouter-nodes-8xkgt 2/2 Running 5 (5d ago) 5d contrail contrail-vrouter-nodes-vrdpdk-ntsvt 1/1 Running 0 5d jcnr kube-crpd-worker-sts-0 1/1 Running 7 (5d ago) 5d jcnr syslog-ng-qz46t 1/1 Running 0 5d kube-system calico-kube-controllers-6dfcdfb99-xbkgt 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h kube-system calico-node-v7pxr 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h kube-system calico-node-zvd7c 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h kube-system coredns-645b46f4b6-6dt4z 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h kube-system coredns-645b46f4b6-fgwr5 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h kube-system dns-autoscaler-659b8c48cb-kl995 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h kube-system kube-apiserver-node1 1/1 Running 3 (5d1h ago) 5d3h kube-system kube-controller-manager-node1 1/1 Running 4 (5d1h ago) 5d3h kube-system kube-multus-ds-amd64-j8rfn 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d2h kube-system kube-multus-ds-amd64-tf2zn 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d2h kube-system kube-proxy-jxb4x 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d1h kube-system kube-proxy-tpx2d 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d1h kube-system kube-scheduler-node1 1/1 Running 2 (5d1h ago) 5d3h kube-system nginx-proxy-node2 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h kube-system nodelocaldns-497z5 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h kube-system nodelocaldns-mqmwj 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h

task.title: Import Cloud-Native Router Images from the Downloaded Software Package (w1)

taskbody.context:
Use this procedure to import Cloud-Native Router images from the downloaded Cloud-Native Router software package to the containerd runtime.
Your cluster can pull Cloud-Native Router images from the enterprise-hub.jnpr.net repository or your cluster can use the Cloud-Native Router images that are included in the downloaded Cloud-Native Router software package.
This latter option is useful if your cluster doesn't have access to the Internet or if you want to set up your own repository.
Setting up your own repository is beyond the scope of this document, but your cluster can still use the included images if you manually import them to the containerd runtime on each cluster node running JCNR. We show you how to do this in the procedure below.
We'll import the images to the containerd runtime on the w1 node only because that's the only node where we're installing JCNR. If you're installing Cloud-Native Router on the control plane node or on other worker nodes, then repeat this procedure on all other nodes where you're installing JCNR.

step.cmd: SSH to the w1 node as root.
Make an images directory to hold the images.
Copy the images from h1.
step.info: Assuming the images are in /root/Juniper_Cloud_Native_Router_24.1-108/images/jcnr-images.tar.gz on h1:scp root@10.16.0.10:/root/Juniper_Cloud_Native_Router_24.1-108/images/jcnr-images.tar.gz ~/images

step.cmd: Gunzip the images package.
Import the images to the containerd runtime.
Check that the images have been imported.

taskbody.context: We'll use basic kubectl commands to check our installation.

step.cmd: Check the pods using the wide option.

info.codeblock: NAMESPACE NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE NOMINATED NODE READINESS GATES contrail-deploy contrail-k8s-deployer-59df6699c4-tgpzk 1/1 Running 0 5d1h 10.16.0.14 node2 <none> <none> contrail contrail-vrouter-nodes-8xkgt 2/2 Running 5 (5d ago) 5d1h 10.16.0.14 node2 <none> <none> contrail contrail-vrouter-nodes-vrdpdk-ntsvt 1/1 Running 0 5d 10.16.0.14 node2 <none> <none> jcnr kube-crpd-worker-sts-0 1/1 Running 7 (5d ago) 5d1h 10.16.0.14 node2 <none> <none> jcnr syslog-ng-qz46t 1/1 Running 0 5d1h 10.16.0.14 node2 <none> <none> kube-system calico-kube-controllers-6dfcdfb99-xbkgt 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h 10.233.75.4 node2 <none> <none> kube-system calico-node-v7pxr 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h 10.16.0.14 node2 <none> <none> kube-system calico-node-zvd7c 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h 10.16.0.11 node1 <none> <none> kube-system coredns-645b46f4b6-6dt4z 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h 10.233.75.3 node2 <none> <none> kube-system coredns-645b46f4b6-fgwr5 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h 10.233.102.132 node1 <none> <none> kube-system dns-autoscaler-659b8c48cb-kl995 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h 10.233.102.131 node1 <none> <none> kube-system kube-apiserver-node1 1/1 Running 3 (5d1h ago) 5d3h 10.16.0.11 node1 <none> <none> kube-system kube-controller-manager-node1 1/1 Running 4 (5d1h ago) 5d3h 10.16.0.11 node1 <none> <none> kube-system kube-multus-ds-amd64-j8rfn 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d2h 10.16.0.14 node2 <none> <none> kube-system kube-multus-ds-amd64-tf2zn 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d2h 10.16.0.11 node1 <none> <none> kube-system kube-proxy-jxb4x 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d2h 10.16.0.11 node1 <none> <none> kube-system kube-proxy-tpx2d 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d2h 10.16.0.14 node2 <none> <none> kube-system kube-scheduler-node1 1/1 Running 2 (5d1h ago) 5d3h 10.16.0.11 node1 <none> <none> kube-system nginx-proxy-node2 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h 10.16.0.14 node2 <none> <none> kube-system nodelocaldns-497z5 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h 10.16.0.14 node2 <none> <none> kube-system nodelocaldns-mqmwj 1/1 Running 1 (5d1h ago) 5d3h 10.16.0.11 node1 <none> <none>

step.cmd: Check the daemonsets.
step.info: kubectl get ds -A NAMESPACE NAME DESIRED CURRENT READY UP-TO-DATE AVAILABLE NODE SELECTOR AGE contrail contrail-vrouter-masters 0 0 0 0 0 <none> 5d contrail contrail-vrouter-masters-vrdpdk 0 0 0 0 0 <none> 5d contrail contrail-vrouter-nodes 1 1 1 1 1 <none> 5d contrail contrail-vrouter-nodes-vrdpdk 1 1 1 1 1 <none> 5d jcnr syslog-ng 1 1 1 1 1 <none> 5d kube-system calico-node 2 2 2 2 2 kubernetes.io/os=linux 5d3h kube-system kube-multus-ds-amd64 2 2 2 2 2 kubernetes.io/arch=amd64 5d2h kube-system kube-proxy 2 2 2 2 2 kubernetes.io/os=linux 5d3h kube-system nodelocaldns 2 2 2 2 2 kubernetes.io/os=linux 5d3h From the output, you can see that there is no contrail-vrouter-masters daemonset because we installed Cloud-Native Router on the worker node only.

step.cmd: Check the statefulsets.
step.info: kubectl get sts -A NAMESPACE NAME READY AGE jcnr kube-crpd-worker-sts 1/1 5d CRPD is implemented as a statefulset.

step.cmd: Access the CRPD pod.
Once you're in the pod, go into CLI mode.
Verify that the CRPD license has been installed successfully.

info.p: If this command does not return a valid license, then go to the next step to add your license.

step.cmd: Add your CRPD license if your license wasn't installed properly.

substep.cmd: Edit your license key to be all on a single line.
substep.info:
Your license file might look like this: Issue Date : yyyy Expiry Date : yyyy HW Serial Number : N/A SSRN : yyyy Feature : yyyy Quantity : 1 License Type : yyyy License Key :
xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx x Create a file that contains the license key on a single line:xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx xxxxxx ...

substep.cmd: Apply your license key.
substep.info: From the CLI, enter license input mode:request system license add terminalThen copy and paste your single-line license key and follow that with a <cr>. Then use <ctrl> d to exit license input mode.

substep.cmd: Confirm that your license was applied.

taskbody.context: The fabric interface is the interface that we want Cloud-Native Router to control. We can configure it using CRPD.

step.cmd: Access the CRPD pod.
Once you're in the pod, go into CLI mode.
Configure the IP address of the fabric interface (enp2s0).
step.info: For example:configset interfaces enp2s0 unit 0 family inet address 10.17.0.14/24commit
